description: A 'whemaki' is an immune-mediated hypersensitivity to an identified agent, supported by the clinical evidence available. A person can report an allergy from their source record without reproducing the diagnostic work in conversation. Ordinary intolerance, toxicity, and an adverse effect keep their own relations because the immune mechanism matters here.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for unvoiced 'wh', then unround into true-mid front 'e'. Close the lips for voiced 'm', carry the sound through the nose, and open into stressed central 'a'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into high-front unrounded 'i'.
usage_notes: A count distinguishes one allergy. Used bare, 'whemaki' presents allergy as a condition. The agent and observed response make the report practical for care; severity and uncertainty qualify it. The exact source record stays available, but self-report still matters when the listener cannot inspect that evidence.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A bounded biological relation supports safety without extending purity language to the person who reports it.

semantic_domains.physical: an immune-mediated bodily response to an agent
